nativeRange.habitat: Shallow sheltered lagoons and reef flats; typically found among rubble and branching coral at depths of 0–10 m

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented eating algae, small crustaceans, copepods, pelagic tunicates, and fish eggs. In captivity tends to accept a wide range of foods including flake, pellet, frozen brine shrimp, Mysis shrimp, and vegetable-based preparations. Generally advised to feed small portions multiple times daily.

summary: A small, intensely blue reef fish from the Indo-Pacific, widely considered one of the hardiest saltwater species available. Generally advised for beginners, though its territorial nature has been known to cause problems with smaller or more docile tankmates.
careNotes:
The Blue Damselfish is widely regarded as one of the hardiest and most beginner-friendly marine fish, tolerating a broad range of water conditions and accepting most prepared foods readily. However, its temperament is commonly described as aggressive and territorial, particularly among adult males, which have been known to harass and injure smaller or more passive tankmates well out of proportion to their modest size (typically 7–8.5 cm). A minimum tank of approximately 100 L (around 25–30 gal) is generally advised for a single specimen; groups of two or more have been reported to require 200 L (55 gal) or more to reduce territorial conflicts.

Sexual dimorphism is present: males display a yellow-to-orange tail and ventral fins (and are often marketed under the trade name 'Orangetail Blue Damselfish'), while females are predominantly blue with transparent fins. The species is non-protogynous.

Lifespan sources diverge considerably: most aquarium care guides report 2–6 years under typical captive conditions, while some sources cite potential longevity up to 15 years in optimally maintained aquariums. The averaged estimate of 2–9 years is used here; reviewers should note the wide spread.

Temperature ranges also reflect a blend of aquarium-hobby recommendations (22–29°C) and FishBase wild-habitat data (25.9–29.3°C); the broader range is retained to accommodate captive tolerance.

The species is generally considered reef-safe; isolated reports of nipping at very small ornamental invertebrates exist but are uncommon. Live rock with hiding crevices is strongly recommended to reduce territorial stress.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has been reported as achievable with a bonded pair. Males establish and defend nesting territories, perform courtship displays, and guard demersal egg clutches until hatching (typically around four days). Larvae are pelagic and notoriously difficult to raise due to their small size and specialized dietary needs; successful captive rearing is considered moderately difficult to advanced. Slightly elevated temperatures in the range of 26–28°C have been noted to promote spawning behavior.

primary.notes: Collection page confirmed species availability and general damselfish care parameters. Individual species product page (404); species-specific data sourced from secondary and tertiary sources.

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog
additional.notes: Corroborated size (~3 in), care level (beginner), omnivore diet, lifespan (2–6 years), and territorial temperament.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning has been achieved in captivity with bonded pairs; males guard demersal egg clutches. Raising pelagic larvae is considered the main challenge due to their small size and specialized feeding requirements.
